speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape 5. I'm with survivor Dr. Fred Katz. I'm Lucy Samorodin, the interviewer. Dr. Katz, we were talking about you getting your PhD, and what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1961. And I finished the work actually about 1960 in Chapel Hill. At that time then, I went to work for-- I had my first teaching job in West Texas, Lubbock, Texas, at Texas Tech. There for the first time I started to teach sociology.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of the things I remember from that experience was at the end of the first semester, a young woman came to me and told me that she now, on the basis of having my course, she know what to do with her life. What was that? She was going to become a missionary in Israel. So is that what I was teaching? I mean, this was my period of sort of denial of being a Jew, of being at home within Judaism. And-- and we were talking about the Bible Belt. And so becoming a missionary is not that strange, but that she got it from me, that was strange. But anyway, it did shake me a little bit. But I was still in total denial about my Judaism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, during that time I became very good friends with a colleague, Clyde Wilson, and his wife. And so I spent a lot of time with them. And they moved on to the University of Missouri after one year. And then they helped me get a job at the University of Missouri the next year, in Columbia, Missouri. And so in 1962, I went to the University of Missouri and stayed there for three years. And then in 1965, I went to the State University of New York in Buffalo. Now, during that time, I had a fairly traditional academic kind of work situation, where I taught courses in sociology, which I thought at the time were important. But right now, I'm not at all sure that they were. But anyway, one of the things that came up for me during my time in Missouri was that-- I was then sort of my mid-30s. And as part of my rejection of my Jewish background, I was dating only non-Jewish women. And I discovered very profoundly that I wasn't getting married. It wasn't working. In those-- at that time, you were not living with somebody you were dating. You either marry or you don't. Well, it was inconceivable for me to marry any of the people I was dating. And yet I was steering clear of Jewish people who-- I thought after a while perhaps I should look into that. So there were very few Jewish people in Missouri for me to tap into.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was one reason why I accepted the job in Buffalo, but also because they invited me. They'd seen something I'd published. The university there invited me to come. So I thought that this could lead to some improvement in my personal life, maybe that I could have a family. So I did go to Buffalo and I did get married to a Jewish person there. I married somebody I met at a conference in Dallas, Texas, so in other words wasn't a Buffalo person. But at least I was getting on to that track of a return to Judaism in my life. And so it so happened that my wife of that period-- we eventually married and eventually got divorced.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, at that point, my wife was interested in moving to Israel. She was doing research as an anthropologist, doing research in Israel. So we agreed while we were getting acquainted to at least spend some time, but it might be a good idea to emigrate to Israel altogether. And this is eventually what we did. That is, we first spent one year in Israel, kind of a sabbatical for me, and during which time we arranged for me to get a professor job at the University of Tel Aviv, in-- starting in 1972. So then we went back-- we were in Israel from '69 to '70, I believe it was, went back for one year to Buffalo, and then sort of wound up my affairs and went to-- emigrated to Israel. What happened in Israel was pretty much of a shock in my life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Israel has a very sophisticated program for teaching people the Hebrew language. It's called Ulpan. It's sort of a total immersion program. That is, you live in a setting where you're given housing and food. And if you have children, you get child support-- child care. All you have to do is learn the language. And for most people, this works very well, for most of the immigrants. The Israelis had lots of experience at teaching people Hebrew and they've worked this out to a fine art. Well, what worked for most people didn't work for me. During the three months or so that we spent in this absorption center during the class, I never spoke. I never opened my mouth. And I simply froze and I didn't learn Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody says, you lived in Israel for five years? You must be perfect in Hebrew. Not me. So something in me balked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, what I learned much later-- several-- about 10 years later was that there have been studies of children who were taken from one country to another and had to learn a new language, one they didn't know before, and were in new settings. Well, most of them learned the language. We have the old stereotype, children learn languages very easily. That's hogwash as far as I'm concerned. Anyway, what had happened for many of these children, they did learn the language and operated in it pretty well. But if later in life they had to learn another new language, then the whole thing broke out into the open and the terror of the little child came out. This is apparently what happened to me. In Israel, I felt like a terrified little child who couldn't understand what people were saying. And I was making it happen. By not learning the language, I was making myself into that child who couldn't understand what people are saying. And so life became increasingly uncomfortable for me. Nobody at the university said you must teach in Hebrew, but it was certainly understood that after a while I would do so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like being surrounded by Jews, almost all Jews and other survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was the other part of this, that is, I had carefully avoided for 20, 30 years all contact with survivors. And in Israel, I was meeting them every day. I was meeting myself every day. I was meeting people with my kind of history. And that proved disconcerting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So emotionally, it was a real roller coaster, despite the fact on a personal level I discovered relatives who were extremely warm to us. And my wife had relatives who were extremely warm to us. So on the personal side, we were given family who were eager to embrace us and were very warm to us. But I created this monster for myself of not learning the language and balking and really in a sense confronting myself or my background. The terror of the little child came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, at the University, I was their first full professor of sociology. And in this European type of system-- the academic system in Israel is patterned after the European-- it's being on the level of cabinet minister to be a full-time-- a full professor with lifetime tenure is a very high status thing in Israel, more so than here. And I was that. I was the only full professor in sociology. And I felt like a terrified little child. I was the department's representative on University Senate. They helped to govern the university. And I couldn't understand what the hell they were saying. And so I was very increasingly uncomfortable, so eventually this led to our return to America. And in a sense, this led to a difficult period for about 10 years there after coming back to America. I came back-- so in a sense-- let me backtrack. As part of my particular inadequate way of coping, to put it gently, I had walked away from a lifetime full professorship twice, once in Israel, once at State University of New York, where I was also a full professor with lifetime tenure. And I walked away each time through my own making to some extent.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I'm trying to get at here is my way of adapting or not adapting afterwards has not been the healthiest. And this I hope will be a lesson for many people. Did you ever revisit your hometown of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oberlauringen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oberlauringen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I previously I think I said I went back in 1979. I think it was wrong. I think it was 1974. That too was part of my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shock treatment of coming to terms with-- yes. I visited it for one day, Oberlauringen. That is, for many-- for years I had sort of the typical survivor's notion that if I set foot in German soil, I will have a heart attack. I can never go back. And then something kept gnawing at me that said, I must go back, I want to go back, I have to go back. I have to go back. And that became stronger and stronger in me that I must go back. Now, I said maybe I can find out something more about the last days of my parents while they were there. Maybe I can find out something. But in reality, I didn't really know what was pulling me or pushing me or driving me. So eventually, I did go back for one day. I was then still married. I was divorced later, but I was still married. And it was really with the help of my wife. I would not have had the courage to go back alone. Anyway, in order to go back, I had to-- I wanted to make some arrangements to-- to contact some people there. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't-- I remembered every Jewish person in the village, what they looked like, how nice they were, how crazy they were, where they sat in the synagogue, how they said their prayers, what work they did, how crazy they were in their personal lives. I could mention stories about each person. I did not remember a single Christian, not a name, not a face, nothing. I wiped them out, despite the fact that we lived next to Christians. We were not a separate ghetto. In the German system, you lived within the village. I could not remember a single Christian. So I asked my sister, my older sister, for names of people in Germany. And she remembered names, schoolmates she'd gone to school with, two women. So I wrote to them beforehand. They wrote back, very warm, very loving, inviting us to come. And so we went. And I made the arrangement I would arrive in the morning and leave in the evening, because I was terrified about staying overnight. I remember the smashed windows overnight. And that's what I eventually did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I arrived in-- we arrived in the morning. We arrived in Frankfurt and then took a-- rented a car to Oberlauringen, which is about two and a half hours from there. So anyway, the two women greeted us very warmly. All day long they were extremely warm and loving, kind, showed us around, where do we want to go? But I didn't remember them. I didn't-- a man came up to me and said, do you remember me? We were the smallest ones in class. We always sat together. I didn't remember him. I didn't tell him, but I didn't remember. I didn't remember anybody, any name or face all day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- throughout the day then we went into my house. And the people who lived there were very gracious and kind. They had not-- they were not the ones who had taken away my parents' house. They'd moved to Oberlauringen and bought this house, so they had no guilt. And it was very evident. They were very open. I could go upstairs, downstairs, in the attic. So I remember going in the attic where I used to play. And somehow, I assumed I would find toys. I didn't. So in other words, I assume my childhood was going to be given back to me. It wasn't. But I blind-- I also assumed that the front of the house would-- that my father's name would still be there, or at least under a coat of paint I could vaguely see Max Katz. No, it wasn't. So I came with these assumptions that my childhood would be returned to me, and it wasn't. I went to the cellar. So the house was totally renovated into modern-- what I think of as modern American suburbia. You could have walked into any house here and be the same, the same furnishings, the television, of course running water by then, electricity, and all the appliances. You could be in modern suburban house, totally different from the past, except that the attic and the cellar had not yet been renovated and they were still the same. I sort of assumed they would come back to me. But anyway, so I went to the house. I went to the school. The school was closed, so we looked-- we looked in through the windows. We went to the cemetery. The Jewish cemetery was in beautiful condition, beautiful. The lawns were beautiful. The stones were all straight and erect. Now, apparently the German government had allocated money to keep the Jewish cemeteries. It was in beautiful condition.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I walked around in the village. And-- and all of a sudden, I was in front of what was formerly a synagogue and was now a bank. And there I stood and I was unable to move. I was unable to go in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remembered-- I guess in retrospect maybe I was remembering the desecration of the Torahs. But here-- and I was this totally non-practicing Jew for 25 years. And here the synagogue wrenched me. I was unable to go in. This was the only building that was available to me I couldn't go in. And then, the other phenomena during that day, in the afternoon, we were invited to an old lady's home over coffee and cake. And I think it was the mother of this person who said we were the smallest in class. And this woman bragged about how nice she had been to my mother. She'd give my mother food in exchange for her silver. And I smiled. I smiled all day. It was only the next day after I'd left it hit me what this woman had said, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that she had robbed my mother. She raped my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But the-- on a healthier note, there was one woman, everybody said you have to see Ernestine. You have to talk to her. It turned out that Ernestine took care of me as a baby, as far as I know, that she diapered me, she later told me. This was a woman who was probably in her 80s when we went there, perhaps 78 or 80. Everybody had said that she stood by the Jews. And apparently she did. That is, after I left, all the Jews had to give up their houses and they all had to move into one house together, those who still remained. And the whole village distanced themselves from the Jews, except this woman who brought-- who really brought them food, Ernestine. And so apparently she was connected to our household somehow. And so when I went to see her, she was a very simple, very uneducated, simple woman, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] except she has a heart. [CRYING] And she showed me pictures and gave me pictures. I still have them. And so she made the difference in that visit. She has since died. I believe she had a daughter who married an American soldier, who moved-- who went to live in Atlanta. But I never found out the name. This was the American zone of occupation where my village lived-- existed, so I think her daughter seemed to have married an American soldier. Anyway, so that was the visit to Oberlauringen, my one-day visit. So I-- after I left, I was totally torn. I mean, on the one hand, there was Ernestine, but I also remembered the other lady, the coffee lady, and how I had the frozen smile on my face when I should have confronted them. And the point of it all was I wasn't ready to confront, and so I wasn't available for healing. What they wanted, they wanted to be forgiven. And I was not able to forgive. But I was also not confronting it. I was only confronting it after I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you left Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So I went-- I was increasingly uncomfortable in Israel, because of my problems, not because of the university or Israel. It was my problem. So I arranged for a sabbatical to be at University of Toronto from 1976 to '77. And I was really interested in finding a job back in America. Well, it so happened I didn't find a job while I was in Toronto. I stayed a second year in Toronto. Eventually, I got a job near Baltimore, at the veterans hospital at Perry Point for heading a research center there. So I went-- we moved to Baltimore, but I failed to get a job in academics. And then I was there in the veterans hospital for three years and I failed in that job. I failed in the most important way for a researcher. I failed to get research grants. So eventually, the center was dissolved and I was left without work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: '81. So then I was basically unemployed for a while. And really, for the next eight years or so, I had-- I had employment of a very minimal sort, working for a foundation here in Baltimore. But I was earning virtually no income. And there was a period where my marriage dissolved. I had a breakdown. I was hospitalized for three weeks for depression. My-- my marriage ended and I was earning virtually no income. And the whole world dissolved in many ways for me. But there was one thing that didn't end. In fact, it began during about that time. I started to address myself to the Holocaust professionally. And then a little later, I started to address myself to it personally. The professional thing was I felt that a good deal of the writings about the Holocaust was not daring enough intellectually, that we were-- that we were assuming that only monsters do monstrous things. And I was starting from the notion that very ordinary people can do monstrous things, but the trick is to explain how this can work, people like ourselves, whereas the party line among survivors, among scholars was the Germans were totally different from the rest of us. Even now we have writings that say their particular brand of anti-Semitism is so unique, nobody else's is like that, or it is the uniqueness of the issue of anti-Semitism that's bad. And I'm saying it is in the character of human beings, ordinary human beings, to use their makeup, the same makeup for good and for evil, can be used for good or for evil, and we can discover how it works. So I felt an intellectual challenge that I felt wasn't being met. And I started to publish articles in 1982. And I kept writing. And in-- eventually in 1996 I published my first book on that, And I've just finished another-- another one is with an agent, and I'm working on a third, really a trilogy on this work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So intellectually, I was finding meaning for my life that involved both confronting the Holocaust but also seeing it as an intellectual challenge that wasn't being met. I also at that time, in the mid-1980s, I joined a Holocaust survivor group, a child survival group in the Baltimore-Washington area, where many of us battled the same battles of confronting the Holocaust. And I'm still a member.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the odd thing was in-- 10 years earlier, in the 1970s, I am convinced none of us would have joined a group like that. But in the mid-'80s, we were all about ready to confront our personal stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to forget?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, in a sense, the reason why I went to my village was to-- to end my beginnings, to forget the beginnings was really the reason why I went back. So in one sense I was trying to forget the horrors, but also trying to clarify-- I was trying to forget them, to end them, and not necessarily to forget. And I don't know whether I succeeded or not. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I would like to forget my fouled-up ways of handling life, my fouled-up ways of handling life, of-- particularly in Israel in relation to my family, I think I was not a good parent to my daughter during the '80s when sort of I was mentally wrapped up in my wrestlings, not that I would like to forget it, I would like to change it if I could, and I can't. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I guess I would like to forget misdirected anger. I would like to-- I wish I had been appropriately angry at times and less inappropriately angry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents were courageous people. They were decent people. They-- they made a huge sacrifice. They had enormous-- I survived because of the courage of my parents and the vision of my parents. I survived because there were people in England who took it upon themselves to help perfectly strange people to whom they had no family connection to. I survived because there were some people along the way who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put their arms around me. I mentioned three women, my mother, Mrs. [INAUDIBLE], and Mrs. Cartwell-- more than one, more than three, four-- who were not-- except for my mother, who were not related to me, who went out of their way to help me in a way a human being helps another human being, not because of anything particular in me, but because they were human beings who showed me what it is to be a human being. So I survived because there are good people in the world and people of character who live up to what it is to be a human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would your message be to your family, to your child, and possibly to the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me start out with to my child, my daughter, that I tried the best I could and I-- I know I've screwed things up along the way in some ways, that I hope she can find joy [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] amid the bumblings of a life that had not the kind of support that many other people have, but also that I've had privileges that I feel good about. To the world, that there's a great deal of unfinished business and a great deal of confusion about some things such as the Holocaust, that we need not remain so impotent against the Holocaust. And I-- I think many of you will be familiar with Emil Fackenheim's famous statement, don't give Hitler a posthumous victory by giving up on Judaism. Well, I'm saying don't give Hitler a posthumous victory by giving up on science. That is the science of human behavior is now about where the physical sciences where 1,000 years ago, that a good deal of our failure to control or to produce more decent human existence is simply because our level of science when it comes to human behavior is just extremely backward as yet, and that the challenge is to be more imaginative and more daring in how we construct our ways of looking at human behavior, and that I believe we can be far more effective than we have been at this date. That is, we are surrounded by impotence when it comes to human affairs, but we don't need to remain so impotent.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has this affected your belief in God, in Judaism, and in religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've deliberately tried to return to Judaism and the belief in God. I've been a member of a congregation for the last 18 years. I've deliberately tried to return and become active in it. I have not turned the corner of finding a nurturing God, but I'm working at it. So in a broad sense, I have gone past the notion of God was absent. I don't think God was absent, but I really don't understand yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to say?
